assets_and_liabilities.rent_or_mortgage_.answer: Spokane Teachers Credit Union Mortgage Services

customer_comments.customer_comments.answer:
I am submitting this Personal Financial Statement in connection with a Spokane Tribal Credit long-term loan application to consolidate some higher-interest debts and finish necessary repairs on our primary residence on the Spokane Reservation.

PERSONAL BACKGROUND
My name is Daniel Joseph Redhawk (DOB: 1984-07-18, SSN: 529-44-1836). I am an enrolled member of the Spokane Tribe of Indians and have lived on or near the reservation for most of my life. My wife, Melissa Anne Redhawk (DOB: 1986-03-02, SSN: 537-62-9142), is acting as my co-signer for this loan. We currently reside at 1423 Riverside Loop, Wellpinit, WA 99040. Our home is a modest 3-bedroom manufactured home on tribal trust land that we purchased in 2018. The land itself is held in trust, but the structure and improvements are owned by us, which is reflected in the home value and mortgage balance listed above.

EMPLOYMENT AND INCOME CONTEXT
I have been employed full-time for the past 7 years as a Facilities Maintenance Technician with the Spokane Tribal Housing Authority. My current gross income averages about $4,150 per month before taxes and deductions, which includes some occasional overtime during winter months for snow removal and emergency repairs. My typical net take-home pay after taxes, retirement, and insurance deductions is around $3,150 per month.

My wife Melissa works part-time as a teaching assistant at the Wellpinit Elementary School and also helps seasonally at the tribal community center for youth programs. Her combined monthly gross income averages about $1,850, with net take-home around $1,450. Between the two of us, our combined net income is approximately $4,600 per month. We do not currently receive any child support, alimony, or public assistance. We have two children, ages 9 and 6, and we budget carefully to manage household expenses, school costs, and some extended family obligations.

DETAILS ON ASSETS
Home: The home present value of $315,000 is based on a comparative market analysis done informally by a local real estate agent in late 2025 for similar manufactured homes on tribal land in our area, as well as recent sales of nearby properties. The outstanding mortgage balance of $242,000 and monthly payment of $1,785 reflect our current mortgage with Spokane Teachers Credit Union Mortgage Services, including principal, interest, and escrow for property taxes. Property taxes on trust land are lower than off-reservation properties, which helps keep the payment manageable.

Vehicles: We own two vehicles. The primary vehicle is a 2018 Subaru Outback (approximate current value $15,500) that we use for commuting into Spokane and for transporting our kids to school and activities. We still owe roughly $8,200 on this auto loan, with a monthly payment of $289. The second vehicle is a 2012 Ford F-150 (approximate value $9,500) that is fully paid off. I use the truck for hauling firewood, doing side jobs like small home repairs for neighbors, and towing a small utility trailer. There is no loan on the truck, so the payment and balance are both $0.

Insurance: We carry homeowner's insurance through a regional insurer that specializes in properties on tribal land. The policy is paid monthly through automatic debit, approximately $92 per month, which is reflected as the home insurance payment. For auto insurance, we insure both vehicles under a single policy. The auto insurance payments present balance of $128 represents our average monthly cost. The policy is billed every 3 months, which is why I marked "true" under the 3-month option and "false" for the other intervals. When the quarterly bill comes due, it is around $384, which we budget for in advance.

Other Assets: In addition to the home and vehicles, I have a small 401(k) retirement account through work with a balance of roughly $11,000 and a savings account with about $3,000 reserved for emergencies. Melissa has a small IRA worth around $4,000 and a checking account that usually holds $1,500–$2,000 depending on the timing of bills. These financial assets, combined with the home equity and vehicle values, are included in the total assets owned by the borrower and co-signer. The total assets figure for me, $340,000, primarily reflects the home value, our two vehicles, and my retirement and cash savings. Melissa’s total assets of $12,000 reflect her IRA, personal savings, and her share of our joint checking.

DETAILS ON LIABILITIES AND DEBTS
Mortgage: As noted above, we owe $242,000 on the home with a monthly payment of $1,785. The loan is a 30-year fixed-rate mortgage at 4.25% interest, originated in 2018. We have never been late on a mortgage payment.

Spokane Tribal Credit Loans: I currently have an existing long-term loan with Spokane Tribal Credit, with a remaining balance of $6,400 and a monthly payment of $210. This loan was originally used to consolidate some older payday loans and to cover closing costs on our home purchase. I also have a smaller auto repair loan through Spokane Tribal Credit with a remaining balance of $1,200 and a monthly payment of $100. That loan was used in 2024 when we had to replace the transmission on the Ford F-150 unexpectedly. I do not currently have any short-term or education loans through Spokane Tribal Credit; those balances and payments are listed as 0.

Credit Cards and Other Debt: Our total credit card and other unsecured debt is approximately $6,100. This includes:
- A Visa card with a balance of about $3,200 at around 18% interest.
- A store credit card (used mainly for school clothes and some household items) with a balance of about $1,400.
- A personal line of credit from our local bank with a balance of approximately $1,500.

Our combined minimum monthly payments on these debts total about $215, which is the number listed for credit cards and other debt payments present balance. We have not missed any payments in the last three years, but the interest rates are relatively high, and that is a major reason why we are seeking to consolidate some of this into a lower-rate loan through Spokane Tribal Credit.

Family Obligations and Support: I am not under any court-ordered alimony or child support obligations, which is why those fields are marked as "N/A." However, in practice, we do regularly help my mother with groceries and utilities. She lives on a fixed income and sometimes struggles to cover heating costs in the winter. Although this is not a formal legal obligation, it is part of our monthly budgeting. On average we spend around $150–$200 per month on support for her, especially during colder months when power bills are higher.

BUDGET AND CASH FLOW
To give a fuller picture, our regular monthly expenses are approximately as follows (rounded):
- Mortgage (P&I, taxes): $1,785
- Home insurance: $92
- Auto loan (Subaru): $289
- Auto insurance: $128
- Spokane Tribal Credit long-term loan: $210
- Spokane Tribal Credit auto repair loan: $100
- Credit cards and other unsecured debt: $215
- Utilities (electricity, propane, water, garbage): $360 (varies with season)
- Phone and internet: $165
- Groceries and household items: $750–$800
- Gas and transportation: $280
- School-related costs (supplies, sports fees, field trips): about $100 on average
- Support for my mother: $150–$200

Altogether, our predictable monthly obligations typically run between $4,200 and $4,400 depending on the season and any unexpected expenses. With a combined net income of around $4,600 per month, our budget is tight but manageable as long as nothing major goes wrong. We have been working hard over the last few years to build a small emergency fund and to pay down the highest-interest debt first.

PURPOSE OF NEW LOAN AND PLANS
The purpose of the new long-term loan we are applying for is to consolidate approximately $6,100 in higher-interest credit card and unsecured debt and to finance around $4,000 in home repairs and improvements. Specifically, we need to:
1. Replace aging roofing materials on the south side of our home where we have had minor leaks during heavy rain. A local contractor provided an estimate of about $2,400 for materials and labor.
2. Upgrade two older exterior doors and weather stripping to improve energy efficiency before next winter (estimated cost around $1,100).
3. Replace a failing hot water heater that is over 15 years old and has started to leak intermittently (estimate about $500–$600 plus installation).

By consolidating the credit card and personal line of credit balances into a lower-interest loan and addressing these home repairs now, we expect our overall monthly payments to be slightly lower and much more predictable. It will also reduce the risk of more costly repairs in the future, such as major water damage from roof leaks or a complete failure of the hot water heater.

CREDIT HISTORY AND PAYMENT BEHAVIOR
In 2016–2017, before we purchased our home, I had some difficulty managing a series of small payday loans and an old credit card from my early twenties. After meeting with a financial counselor through the tribal community center and talking with Spokane Tribal Credit, I entered into a repayment plan and used a previous tribal credit loan to consolidate those debts. Since then, I have not taken out any new payday loans, and we have focused on building a more stable financial base. We have not had any late payments reported on our mortgage, auto loans, or tribal credit loans in the past five years.

We are actively working on improving our credit by keeping utilization down on our remaining cards, making more than the minimum payment when possible, and avoiding new unnecessary debt. I check my credit report once a year and dispute any errors. We also attend the financial literacy workshops offered by Spokane Tribal Credit when our schedules allow, which have helped us with budgeting, understanding interest rates, and planning for emergencies.

EXPLANATION OF TOTALS AND ANY APPARENT DISCREPANCIES
You may notice that the total assets and total liabilities listed for me (the borrower) do not match our full household net worth exactly. This is because some accounts are legally in Melissa’s name, some are joint, and the form breaks out totals for borrower and co-signer separately. The total assets I listed for myself ($340,000) include the full home value, vehicles, and my individual retirement and savings. Melissa’s total assets ($12,000) reflect her personal financial accounts and her share of our joint checking. Similarly, some liabilities like the mortgage are in both our names but are primarily assigned under my borrower section for the sake of this application.

If there is any concern about the totals or if you need a more detailed breakdown (for example, the specific credit card issuers, account numbers, or exact balances as of the date of this application), I can provide recent statements from all lenders and our bank. I can also provide pay stubs, W-2 forms, and tax returns for the last two years to verify income.

LANGUAGE AND COMMUNICATION PREFERENCES
Our primary language at home is English, although we are both making an effort to learn and use more of the Spokane Salish language with our children. For any official loan documents or complex financial explanations, we prefer written English and the option to sit down with a loan officer in person or via video call to review terms and ask questions. I am comfortable reading and signing electronic documents as well.

In case it is helpful for follow-up, my contact information is:
- Phone (cell): (509) 555-2749
- Alternate phone (Melissa): (509) 555-6382
- Email (primary): daniel.redhawk84@example.com
- Email (Melissa): melissa.redhawk@example.com

We understand that approval is not guaranteed and that this statement must be accurate and complete. To the best of my knowledge, the information provided here reflects our current financial situation as of the date signed below. If anything changes materially (for example, a job change, a significant new debt, or a major change in housing status), we will notify Spokane Tribal Credit promptly.

Thank you for taking the time to review this detailed explanation. I am available to answer any questions about our income, expenses, or the specific items listed in the Assets and Liabilities section.
